author: @martinwinfield2935
text: Thanks for the video, the coil measurement for the first layer will be that length but the rest of the turns will be longer with each layer. So with that measured length surly you will end up with less turns. Just a point of view.

author: @totherarf
text:
If you have a long length of wire there is a shortcut you can use!

Do it exactly as you did ..... but when you measure the wire start at the middle and double the number of turns with each lap! Now there is a problem doing this .... the coil now runs half the turns in one direction and half in the other (no good for creating a magnetic field) . So you employ a simple trick. You have now got the two ends together and a short loop at the opposite end. Simply cut the loop and join one end to the opposite piece from the loop you just cut. 
You end up with one end spiraling round to the cut loop and back to the start of the other piece. You do need to check you join the right end to the right piece you cut (a meter is handy ... or if you are stuck a torch bulb and a battery will do). It is a bit long winded to explain , but will make sense if you try it! ;0)

author: @kreynolds1123
text:
Tip when wrapping many turns a toroid.  get a thin but comparitivly long stick that will pass through the center. in your case a 10cm stick would be nice. Notch the top and bottom of the stick and wind the wire length you determined between the top and bottom notches. 8 meters of wire on a 10cm stick is  20 cm per turn meaning just 40 turns in total. 

Now the stick also known as a shuttle will carry the whole length of the wire through the center of the toroid. The shuttle greatly shortens the length of wire you have to fiddle with at any one moment.
